title: Intmax
description: A stateless zkRollup optimised for private payments, pushing most data off-chain so that on-chain costs are minimal while preserving user-side privacy through client-side proving.

properties.name: Confidentiality
properties.notes: Users construct their own transaction batches locally. These batches consist of arbitrary mappings from recipients to token amounts and are not revealed to aggregators. Each transaction batch is salted and hashed. Only these hashes are submitted to the aggregator, preserving privacy. The transaction data stays in the user device while the transaction hash is sent to the aggregator and later published on-chain.

properties.name: Anonymity
properties.notes: Sender and receiver are hidden when a transaction is submitted in the L2. When a sender wants to send a transaction he needs to compute a ZK proof showing he has enough balance. The ZK proof and the transaction details are send to the receiver so he can update his balance in his local state (end-user device). The transaction hashes are sent to the aggregator to publish the newest state on-chain.

properties.name: Asset privacy
properties.notes: When users deposit assets into or withdraw assets from the Intmax network the token type is recorded on Ethereum L1. There is no asset privacy when entering or leaving the protocol. Inside the L2 the private transfers do not reveal the asset being transmitted, only salted hashes of the transactions are published on-chain.

properties.name: Plausible deniability
properties.notes: Deposits from Ethereum to Intmax are submitted as standard L1 transactions to the Intmax Rollup contract, making it publicly visible on-chain that a user is entering the Intmax L2 network. Internal private transfers could be plausible denied because only salted hashes of the transactions are submitted. No transaction details like sender, recipient, amount and transfer metadata get published on the L2.

properties.name: Number of secrets
properties.notes: Users need their Ethereum wallet key (secret #1) to interact with the L1 contracts and a separate Intmax private key (secret #2) to authorize transfers within the network.

properties.name: Time-to-finality
properties.notes: INTMAX is a stateless zkRollup on top of Ethereum and Scroll. Block builders can submit Intmax blocks to Scroll at any time but they are rewarded to do so frequently. Hence we assume that Intmax block get published at the transaction execution time. In this case we need to account for Scroll finality (submitting valid ZK proofs of the data published in the Data Availability Layer of the L1) that at the time of writing is approximately 43 minutes. On top of that we need to add Ethereum L1 block time finality which is 12 minutes. In total we have approximately 55 minutes for finality time of a private transfer in Intmax

properties.name: Deposit time
properties.notes: It takes about 30 minutes for a Ethereum L1 to Intmax deposit to be reflected. This is related to the time it takes the block builders to publish the new state root with the new private balance of the user.

properties.name: Withdraw time
properties.notes: It takes about 6 hours for an Intmax to Ethereum L1 withdrawal transaction to be reflected. This is related to the withdrawal server or withdrawal aggregator time it takes to verify the withdrawal zk proof, post the data on-chain (waiting for hard finality) and transferring funds to the public recipients.

properties.name: Censorship resistance
properties.notes: The Intmax Liquidity contract is managed by a designated operator, which means access to deposits and withdrawals is gated by a centralised entity. This design introduces a single point of censorship at the L1 gateway that could block or delay user transactions. For example Intmax deposits and withdrawals were paused due to a ZK bug.

properties.name: External network dependence
properties.notes: Intmax relies on a network of external nodes (block builders, aggregators, and validity provers) to process and settle transactions. These roles are permissionless, meaning anyone can run the required services and participate in the network without needing approval from the core team.

properties.name: Escape hatch
properties.notes: Withdrawals from Intmax require the block builder to include the withdrawal in a batch and the validity proof to be submitted on-chain. Withdrawals from L2 to L1 require the user to submit a ZK proof of balance at a known commitment root. This root must exist in the contract’s on-chain history.

properties.name: Upgradeability
properties.notes: Intmax smart contracts are currently controlled by a single admin account with no on-chain governance or token-based voting mechanism. Until a decentralised governance model is introduced, all protocol upgrades depend on unilateral decisions by the core team.

properties.name: Client-side proving
properties.notes: ZK proofs for Intmax transactions are generated entirely on the user's device inside the supported wallet applications or web interfaces. This means no external prover service has access to private transaction data. The aggregators only receive salted hashes of the transactions, all transaction details are kept at the users' devices.

properties.name: Third-party inspectability
properties.notes: Transaction contents are end-to-end encrypted and proven with ZK proofs, so neither the network operators (including aggregators) nor any external observer can read private transfer data. All transaction data stays on users' end devices, regular monthly backups should be executed (automatically or manually) to prevent data loss.

properties.name: Implementation maturity
properties.notes: Intmax launched on Ethereum mainnet in June 2025

properties.name: Post-quantum secure
properties.notes: Intmax uses Plonky2 for its ZK proofs. Plonky2 is considered post quantum secure because it is hash-based unlike other proving systems like Groth16 or Plonk that are based on Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC).

properties.name: Layer of enforcement
properties.notes: AML compliance is enforced on the privacy mining feature designed to increase the anonymity set (deposit tokens into Intmax, wait a time period, withdraw to a different address). The L2 will block deposits coming from high-risk addresses and it will require a proof of innocence to allow deposits

properties.name: Enforcement entities
properties.notes: Compliance screening is performed by third-party AML service providers that supply the block-lists and risk-scoring data consumed by the Intmax protocol at deposit time.

properties.name: Type of compliance
properties.notes: Intmax applies Know Your Transaction (KYT) screening to incoming deposits and uses a Proof of Innocence (POI) model whereby users must demonstrate that their funds are not associated with sanctioned or illicit activity before being accepted into the network.

properties.name: Point of enforcement
properties.notes: AML and compliance checks are applied exclusively at the deposit stage when users bridge assets from Ethereum L1 into the Intmax network. Once funds are inside the network, subsequent transfers are not subject to additional compliance screening. Withdrawals are assumed to be clean if no bad actor has deposited into the L2.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing entity
properties.notes: Intmax does not provide any viewing-key or selective-disclosure mechanism. Transaction history is accessible only through the user's own account. Users have the responsibility to regularly backup their data every month to prevent data loss and missing funds.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing control
properties.notes: There is no viewing-key system or programmable disclosure control in Intmax. Users cannot scope or share access to their transaction history with any external party. A  user could explicitly share a specific transaction to an external party by sharing the transaction details, ZK proof and the on-chain reference storage.

properties.name: Cryptographic verifiability
properties.notes: Intmax uses ZK proofs to cryptographically guarantee the validity of every state transition (aka transfers). The architecture principles include formal verification of the circuits, ensuring that correctness is enforced by mathematics rather than by trusted intermediaries.

properties.name: Open source
properties.notes: The Intmax smart contracts, node software, and related tooling are publicly available in their open-source code repository, allowing anyone to inspect, audit, or contribute to the codebase.

properties.name: Scalable state
properties.notes: Intmax has a special L2 design similar to edge blockchains. Users are first-class agents who fully own and maintain their own state. Aggregators submit commitments on the L1 for global state coordination.

properties.name: Client-side indexing
properties.notes: Transactions are shared peer-to-peer from the sender to the recipient. The recipient needs to verify a ZK proof in order to update its own client-side state. Only salted hashes of the transactions are published on-chain in order to help receivers get track of the global state and the unused notes (UTXOS).

properties.name: Private state model
properties.notes: Intmax represents private balances as a set of UTXOs. Each received transfer creates a new unspent output controlled by the recipient, and spending requires consuming existing UTXOs and creating new ones.

properties.name: On-chain storage
properties.notes: Intmax minimises on-chain footprint by storing only block roots and validity proofs on Ethereum L1. All transaction data is kept off-chain and propagated through the P2P network, with on-chain commitments serving as the source of truth for finality.

properties.name: Access to DeFi
properties.notes: Currently Intmax is designed as a private payment L2 network. At the moment the native DeFi ecosystem is small and Intmax does not give full access to Ethereum L1 DeFi ecosystem directly. Users must withdraw to Ethereum L1 to access any DeFi applications.

properties.name: Programmability / Generality
properties.notes: Intmax allows for integration into different applications and protocols. The ecosystem is still growing but it looks like Intmax can give programmability features to developers in order to build apps more complex than private transfers
